mute-swan.size: 140–160 cm body length; one of the largest waterfowl
mute-swan.habitat: Lakes, rivers, and coastal waters across Europe and parts of Asia; introduced widely in North America and Australasia
mute-swan.diet: Primarily aquatic plants; also small invertebrates
mute-swan.migration: Largely resident; some northerly birds move south in winter

common-loon.size: Large diving bird; males significantly heavier than females
common-loon.habitat: Breeds on lakes and waterways in Canada, northern USA, Greenland, and Iceland; winters on both coasts of North America and the Atlantic coast of Europe
common-loon.diet: Fish, crustaceans, insect larvae, molluscs, and occasionally aquatic plants
common-loon.migration: Migratory; moves from inland breeding lakes to coastal wintering areas before ice forms in autumn

american-crow.size: Large passerine; smaller than a common raven, larger than a fish crow
american-crow.habitat: Widespread across North America in almost all habitats except tundra
american-crow.diet: Omnivorous — insects, seeds, nuts, small animals, and occasionally carrion
american-crow.migration: Largely resident, though northern populations may shift south in winter

eurasian-nuthatch.size: Small passerine; short-tailed with a long bill
eurasian-nuthatch.habitat: Mature deciduous or mixed woodland with large old trees, preferring oak
eurasian-nuthatch.diet: Mainly insects (especially caterpillars and beetles); supplements with nuts and seeds in autumn and winter
eurasian-nuthatch.migration: Resident; pairs hold permanent territories year-round

eurasian-treecreeper.size: Small passerine; mouse-sized, with a long curved bill and stiff tail
eurasian-treecreeper.habitat: Woodlands of all kinds; favours coniferous forests or higher altitudes where it overlaps with the short-toed treecreeper
eurasian-treecreeper.diet: Insectivorous; picks insects from crevices in bark while climbing tree trunks
eurasian-treecreeper.migration: Largely resident; solitary in winter, occasionally forms communal roosts in cold weather

winter-wren.habitat: Coniferous forests for breeding; winters in dense underbrush across southeastern Canada and the eastern United States
winter-wren.diet: Insects and other invertebrates gleaned from the ground in thick underbrush
winter-wren.migration: Migratory; breeds in coniferous forests from British Columbia to the Atlantic, winters across southeastern Canada and the eastern USA

sarus-crane.size: Up to 1.8 m tall; the tallest flying bird in the world
sarus-crane.habitat: Open wetlands, seasonally flooded forests in Southeast Asia, and Eucalyptus woodlands and grasslands in Australia
sarus-crane.diet: Roots, tubers, insects, crustaceans, and small vertebrates foraged from marshes and shallow wetlands
sarus-crane.migration: Nonmigratory; largely resident throughout its range in the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia, and northern Australia

demoiselle-crane.size: Medium-sized crane; the smallest crane species
demoiselle-crane.habitat: Central Eurosiberian steppes and grasslands, ranging from the Black Sea to Mongolia and Northeast China
demoiselle-crane.diet: Grains, seeds, insects, and small invertebrates (typical crane omnivore diet)
demoiselle-crane.migration: Strongly migratory; western birds winter in Africa, eastern birds winter in the Indian subcontinent

black-headed-ibis.size: Medium wading bird with a long down-curved black bill
black-headed-ibis.habitat: Wetlands, paddy fields, and a range of natural and man-made habitats across South and Southeast Asia
black-headed-ibis.diet: Invertebrates, small fish, and other wetland prey foraged by probing with its curved bill
black-headed-ibis.migration: Partially migratory; breeds seasonally during the rainy season, with some populations dispersing after breeding

indian-white-eye.habitat: Open woodland on the Indian subcontinent
indian-white-eye.diet: Nectar and small insects, foraging in small groups

pheasant-tailed-jacana.size: Medium-sized wading bird with extremely elongated toes and nails
pheasant-tailed-jacana.habitat: Shallow tropical lakes and wetlands with floating vegetation, from Yemen east to the Philippines
pheasant-tailed-jacana.diet: Mainly invertebrates, foraged by walking on aquatic vegetation, swimming, or wading
pheasant-tailed-jacana.migration: Partially migratory; the only jacana that migrates long distances, with seasonal movements across parts of tropical Asia

crested-caracara.size: Medium-large raptor in the falcon family
crested-caracara.habitat: Highly adaptable; found in semi-arid and desert climates, coastal areas, tropical forests, plains, swamps, and urban areas across the Americas
crested-caracara.diet: Opportunistic omnivore; carrion, insects, small vertebrates, and other prey
crested-caracara.migration: Largely resident throughout its range from the southern USA through Latin America to Tierra del Fuego
